perturbation.original_text: Heritage shall produce the products described on Schedule A and Schedule A-1 attached hereto, as may be amended by the Parties hereafter from time to time (the "Products," each individual unit of Product "Unit"), for Premier at [***] Heritage's or Heritage's wholly owned subsidiary, Jasper's facilities (the "Facilities").
perturbation.changed_text: Heritage shall produce the products described on Schedule A and Schedule A-1 attached hereto, as may be amended by the Parties hereafter from time to time (the "Products," each individual unit of Product "Good"), for Premier at [***] Heritage's or Heritage's wholly owned subsidiary, Jasper's facilities (the "Facilities").
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Unit' describing each individual unit of Product has been changed to 'Good'. This introduces ambiguity as 'Unit' is more specific to manufacturing output, while 'Good' has broader implications under commercial law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - § 2-105. Goods; 'Future' Goods; 'Lot'; 'Commercial Unit'.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 2-105 defines 'goods' broadly as all things movable at the time of identification to the contract for sale. Using 'Good' instead of 'Unit' could potentially broaden the scope beyond the originally intended manufactured items, creating disputes under contract law. The original text specifically defines 'Unit' as an individual part of product whereas the replacement 'Good' is broader.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-105. Definitions: Transferability; "Goods"; "Future" Goods; "Lot"; "Commercial Unit". | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-105. Definitions: Transferability; "Goods"; "Future" Goods; "Lot"; "Commercial Unit". (1) " Goods " means all things (including specially manufactured goods) which are movable at the time of identification to the contract for sale other than the money in which the price is to be paid, investment securities (Article 8) and things in action. "Goods" also includes the unborn young of animals and growing crops and other identified things attached to realty as described in the section on goods to be severed from realty (Section 2-107 ). (2) Goods must be both existing and identified before any interest in them can pass. Goods which are not both existing and identified are " future " goods. A purported present sale of future goods or of any interest therein operates as a contract to sell. (3) There may be a sale of a part interest in existing identified goods. (4) An undivided share in an identified bulk of fungible goods is sufficiently identified to be sold although the quantity of the bulk is not determined. Any agreed proportion of such a bulk or any quantity thereof agreed upon by number, weight or other measure may to the extent of the seller's interest in the bulk be sold to the buyer who then becomes an owner in common. (5) " Lot " means a parcel or a single article which is the subject matter of a separate sale or delivery, whether or not it is sufficient to perform the contract . (6) " Commercial unit " means such a unit of goods as by commercial usage is a single whole for purposes of sale and division of which materially impairs its character or value on the market or in use. A commercial unit may be a single article (as a machine) or a set of articles (as a suite of furniture or an assortment of sizes) or a quantity (as a bale, gross, or carload) or any oth
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the term 'Unit' to 'Good' when describing individual products. UCC 2-105 defines 'Goods' broadly as 'all things (including specially manufactured goods) which are movable at the time of identification to the contract for sale'. The original text defines 'Unit' as an individual part of a product which is more specific and less ambiguous. By using 'Good', the contract opens itself to a broader interpretation, potentially including items not originally intended, creating a contradiction through misaligned terminology.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term, Premier shall have the right (but not the obligation) to order from Heritage quantities of Products in in excess of [***] and provided Heritage has the capacity and the ability to produce such additional quantities of Products, Heritage agrees to produce such additional quantities per the pricing and terms on Schedule C.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term, Premier shall have the right (but not the obligation) to order from Heritage quantities of Products in in excess of [***] and provided Heritage desires, Heritage agrees to produce such additional quantities per the pricing and terms on Schedule C.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the phrase 'has the capacity and the ability to produce' with 'desires' changes the obligation to fulfill orders. The original text suggests an objective standard of capacity and ability, whereas the altered text introduces a subjective element of desire, which may not be legally enforceable. Capacity and ability are more objective and measurable terms than desire.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 2-306 implies a good faith obligation based on measurable output or requirements. Changing the trigger for production from 'capacity and ability' to 'desire' introduces a subjective element that could violate the implied obligation of good faith as defined by the UCC, making the agreement potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith , except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the condition under which Heritage is obligated to produce additional quantities of products. The original text uses 'capacity and the ability to produce,' which implies an objective, measurable standard. The changed text uses 'desires,' introducing a subjective element. UCC 2-306, as reflected in Scraped Law Snippet 1, implies a good faith obligation based on measurable output, stating that quantity is measured by 'such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith.' The shift to 'desires' could allow Heritage to avoid fulfilling orders based on preference rather than actual capacity, potentially violating the good faith requirement implied by UCC 2-306.

perturbation.original_text: Heritage shall purchase all ingredients and packaging materials identified in Schedule C to be used in connection with the manufacturer of the Products. Heritage shall invoice Premier through the [***] billing as identified on Schedule C.
perturbation.changed_text: Heritage shall purchase all ingredients and packaging materials identified in Schedule C to be used in connection with the manufacturer of the Products. Heritage shall invoice Premier through the [***] billing, subject to Premier's satisfaction, as identified on Schedule C.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'subject to Premier's satisfaction' to the invoicing process introduces a subjective element that contradicts standard commercial practice. It implies that Premier can arbitrarily refuse to pay invoices, regardless of whether the products were manufactured according to the agreed-upon specifications.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - § 2-606. What Constitutes Acceptance of Goods.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 2-606 defines acceptance of goods based on inspection and failure to reject. Adding 'subject to Premier's satisfaction' allows Premier to reject payment even after accepting the goods, contradicting the UCC's provisions on acceptance and payment obligations. This makes the financial part of the agreement uncertain and legally dubious.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-606. What Constitutes Acceptance of Goods. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-606. What Constitutes Acceptance of Goods. (1) Acceptance of goods occurs when the buyer (a) after a reasonable opportunity to inspect the goods signifies to the seller that the goods are conforming or that he will take or retain them in spite of their non-conformity; or (b) fails to make an effective rejection (subsection (1) of Section 2-602 ), but such acceptance does not occur until the buyer has had a reasonable opportunity to inspect them; or (c) does any act inconsistent with the seller's ownership; but if such act is wrongful as against the seller it is an acceptance only if ratified by him. (2) Acceptance of a part of any commercial unit is acceptance of that entire unit. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces 'subject to Premier's satisfaction' into the invoicing process. This creates a subjective contingency that allows Premier to potentially avoid payment even if Heritage fulfills its manufacturing obligations. This contradicts the standard commercial practice where acceptance of goods, as defined in UCC 2-606, triggers a payment obligation. The added condition undermines the certainty of the financial aspect of the agreement, creating a misaligned terminology that deviates from legally recognized definitions related to acceptance and payment, making the contract potentially unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Acceptance of goods occurs when the buyer...signifies to the seller that the goods are conforming or that he will take or retain them in spite of their non-conformity'. The modification allows for rejection based on 'satisfaction' even after acceptance, which is a contradiction. Since the definition of misaligned terminology involves situations where language deviates from legally recognized definitions, the addition of 'subject to Premier's satisfaction' directly contradicts standard commercial practices and the UCC provision on acceptance.
